meaning: Too little, too late. Help or action that comes when it is no longer useful.
origin: Historical reference to the Holy Brotherhood (Santa Hermandad), rural police who arrived too late to prevent crimes. Their green sleeves (mangas verdes) became a symbol of belated intervention.
why_it_matters: Used ironically when help or a solution arrives after the problem has passed.

canonical: a quien madruga dios le ayuda
meaning: The early bird catches the worm. God helps those who rise early.
origin: Spanish proverb attested since the medieval period. Reflects the agricultural value of rising at dawn.

variants.surface: a quien madruga dios le ayuda
el que madruga dios le ayuda

canonical: al pan pan y al vino vino
meaning: Call a spade a spade. Speak plainly and directly.
origin: Ancient proverb found in Cervantes and earlier sources. The parallelism (bread is bread, wine is wine) insists on calling things by their true name.

variants.surface: al pan pan y al vino vino
llamar al pan pan y al vino vino

meaning: Look for trouble; overcomplicate something that is simple.
origin: A cat has four paws; looking for only three means ignoring reality and creating unnecessary difficulty.
why_it_matters: Used as a warning not to overthink or complicate straightforward matters.

meaning: Rain cats and dogs; pour down heavily.
origin: Chuzo is a pointed pike or spike. Raining pointed spikes emphasizes the violence and intensity of the downpour.

variants.note: With llover (to rain) instead of caer.

canonical: coger el toro por los cuernos
meaning: Take the bull by the horns; deal with a difficult problem head-on.

variants.surface: coger el toro por los cuernos
agarrar el toro por los cuernos
variants.note: agarrar preferred in Latin America over coger.

meaning: Hit the nail on the head; be exactly right.
origin: Carpentry metaphor: hitting the nail precisely versus bending it. Cervantes used it in Don Quijote.

variants.surface: dar en el clavo con algo

meaning: In one ear and out the other; pay no attention to advice.
origin: A broken sack (saco roto) cannot hold what is put in it — advice or information poured in leaks straight out.

canonical: el que ríe último ríe mejor
meaning: He who laughs last laughs best.

variants.surface: el que ríe último ríe mejor

canonical: en boca cerrada no entran moscas
meaning: Silence is golden; loose lips sink ships. Keeping quiet avoids trouble.
origin: Flies cannot enter a closed mouth — silence prevents unwanted intrusions and regrettable words.

variants.surface: en boca cerrada no entran moscas

meaning: Have one's head in the clouds; be daydreaming or distracted.

variants.note: andar instead of estar; implies ongoing state.

meaning: Put on a brave face; summon courage despite fear or difficulty.
origin: Transform intestines (tripas, associated with fear and nausea) into a heart (corazón, associated with courage). Making bravery out of fear.

meaning: Talk an ear off; be a chatterbox; talk excessively.
origin: Speaking through one's elbows (codos) implies uncontrolled, overflowing speech that comes from every part of the body.

meaning: Get to the point; cut to the chase.
origin: Grain (grano) is the essential part of wheat after the husk is removed. Going to the grain means getting to the essential matter.

variants.note: Let's get to the point (first person plural).

canonical: la gota que colmó el vaso
meaning: The straw that broke the camel's back; the final thing that made a bad situation unbearable.
origin: A drop that overflows a full glass. The vessel was already full; any additional drop causes overflow.

variants.surface: la gota que colmó el vaso
la gota que derramó el vaso
variants.note: With derramar (to spill) instead of colmar.

meaning: Things come in threes; bad luck or events tend to happen three times.
Take the blame; be the scapegoat; pay for someone else's mistakes.
origin: Paying for broken plates even when you did not break them — suffering the consequences of others' actions.

variants.note: Separate but related idiom also meaning to take the blame.

meaning: Lose one's cool; lose self-control; go off the rails.
origin: Losing one's papers (documents) means losing one's identity or credentials. Metaphorically, losing control of oneself.

canonical: poner los puntos sobre las íes
meaning: Dot the i's and cross the t's; be precise and leave nothing to chance.

variants.surface: poner los puntos sobre las íes
poner los puntos sobre las ies

meaning: Where there is a will there is a way.

canonical: salir el tiro por la culata
meaning: Backfire; have the opposite of the intended effect.
origin: A gun that fires backward through the stock (culata) instead of forward harms the shooter. Plans that backfire follow the same logic.

variants.surface: salir el tiro por la culata
le salió el tiro por la culata
variants.note: Third person — it backfired on him/her.

meaning: Be thick as thieves; be inseparable.
origin: Nail (uña) and flesh (carne) are so close they cannot be separated without pain.

canonical: tener la sartén por el mango
meaning: Hold the reins; call the shots; be the one in control.
origin: Holding the handle (mango) of a frying pan (sartén) gives control over its direction. Whoever holds the handle decides where it goes.

variants.surface: tener la sartén por el mango
llevar la sartén por el mango

meaning: Throw in the towel; give up; admit defeat.
origin: Boxing term: a trainer throws a towel into the ring to stop a fight and concede defeat.

meaning: Pull someone's leg; tease or make fun of someone.
origin: Taking someone's hair (pelo) is an annoyance and mockery. Metaphorically, to mess with someone playfully or deceitfully.

canonical: ver los toros desde la barrera
meaning: Watch from the sidelines; stay safe while others take risks.
origin: Bullfighting metaphor: watching from behind the protective barrier (barrera) rather than entering the ring with the bull.

variants.surface: ver los toros desde la barrera

meaning: Live off one's laurels; rely on past achievements without new effort.
origin: Living off rental income (rentas) without working — extended metaphorically to coasting on past successes.

meaning: Keep smiling in adversity; put on a brave face when things go wrong.

variants.surface: ante el mal tiempo buena cara

meaning: Go from bad to worse; deteriorate steadily.
Be left wanting; not get what you hoped for.

variants.note: First person past — I was left wanting.

meaning: Not get anything right; mess everything up.
origin: Football/ball-game metaphor: not even managing to kick (dar pie) the ball (bola) — missing completely.

variants.note: First person — I can't get anything right.

meaning: Dodge the question; avoid responsibility; deflect blame.
origin: Football term: kicking the ball out of bounds to relieve pressure. Metaphorically, evading a difficult topic.

canonical: poner el dedo en la llaga
meaning: Put one's finger on the sore spot; touch on a painful truth.
origin: Biblical reference to doubting Thomas touching Christ's wounds (llagas). Pointing to the exact painful or sensitive issue.

variants.surface: poner el dedo en la llaga

variants.note: Shorter form — to touch the wound.

canonical: a falta de pan buenas son tortas
meaning: Beggars can't be choosers; make do with what you have.
origin: If there is no bread, corn cakes (tortas) are welcome. Accept what is available.

variants.surface: a falta de pan buenas son tortas
